description: Doctoral-level psychology research methods pack for design logic, measurement, inference, reproducibility, mixed methods, and evidence grading.

coreConcepts.name: From Research Question to Design
coreConcepts.oneLiner: Match the question type (describe, predict, explain) to the design that can actually answer it.
keyPoints: Descriptive questions → surveys/observational description.
Predictive questions → correlational models; avoid causal wording.
Causal questions → experiments or quasi-experiments with strong identification.
Feasibility and ethics constrain design choice.
commonMisconceptions: Correlation proves cause.
One study settles a question.
Bigger sample automatically fixes bad measurement.

keywords: Match the question type (describe, predict, explain) to the design that can actually answer it
intentSignals: what is from research question to design
define from research question to design
explain from research question to design

definitionSurface.definition: Match the question type (describe, predict, explain) to the design that can actually answer it.
acceptedPrompts: What is From Research Question to Design?
Define From Research Question to Design.
Explain From Research Question to Design.

coreConcepts.name: Variables and Operationalization
coreConcepts.oneLiner: A construct is an idea; an operational definition is how you measure it in the real world.
keyPoints: Define constructs (e.g., anxiety) and specify measurable indicators.
Operational definitions must be observable, repeatable, and documented.
Use multiple measures (triangulation) when feasible.
commonMisconceptions: A single questionnaire score equals the construct.
Changing a label changes what you measured.

keywords: A construct is an idea; an operational definition is how you measure it in the real world

definitionSurface.definition: A construct is an idea; an operational definition is how you measure it in the real world.

coreConcepts.name: Reliability vs Validity
coreConcepts.oneLiner: Reliability is consistency; validity is truthfulness to the construct. You need both.
keyPoints: Reliability types: test-retest, inter-rater, internal consistency.
Validity types: content, criterion, construct; convergent/discriminant.
Reliable but invalid measures can be consistently wrong.
commonMisconceptions: High reliability implies high validity.
Cronbach’s alpha alone proves a scale is good.

keywords: Reliability is consistency; validity is truthfulness to the construct
intentSignals: how do we know it’s valid

definitionSurface.definition: Reliability is consistency; validity is truthfulness to the construct. You need both.

coreConcepts.name: Sampling and Generalizability
coreConcepts.oneLiner: What you can conclude depends on who you studied and how they were selected.
keyPoints: Random sampling supports population generalization; convenience samples don’t.
Selection bias occurs when inclusion is related to variables of interest.
External validity: does this result travel to other settings/people/times?
commonMisconceptions: University student samples represent everyone.
Large convenience samples are automatically generalizable.

keywords: What you can conclude depends on who you studied and how they were selected

definitionSurface.definition: What you can conclude depends on who you studied and how they were selected.

coreConcepts.name: Confounds, Controls, and Randomization
coreConcepts.oneLiner: A confound is an alternative explanation; good design anticipates and blocks it.
keyPoints: Confounds threaten internal validity.
Random assignment balances confounds on average.
Control variables help, but can introduce bias if chosen poorly.
commonMisconceptions: Controlling for everything is always better.
Random sampling is the same as random assignment.

keywords: A confound is an alternative explanation; good design anticipates and blocks it
intentSignals: what is confounds, controls, and randomization

definitionSurface.definition: A confound is an alternative explanation; good design anticipates and blocks it.
acceptedPrompts: What is Confounds, Controls, and Randomization?

coreConcepts.name: Ethics in Psychological Research
coreConcepts.oneLiner: Ethical research minimizes harm, uses informed consent, and protects privacy—especially with vulnerable groups.
keyPoints: Informed consent, right to withdraw, and debriefing when deception is used.
Minimize risk; benefits must justify remaining risk.
Confidentiality and data protection; special care with sensitive topics.
Institutional review processes (IRB/REB) enforce standards.
commonMisconceptions: If data is anonymous, ethics doesn’t matter.
Deception is always unethical (it can be allowed with safeguards).

keywords: Ethical research minimizes harm, uses informed consent, and protects privacy—especially with vulnerable groups
intentSignals: what is ethics in psychological research

definitionSurface.definition: Ethical research minimizes harm, uses informed consent, and protects privacy—especially with vulnerable groups.
acceptedPrompts: What is Ethics in Psychological Research?

coreConcepts.name: Replication and Open Science
coreConcepts.oneLiner: Reliable knowledge needs replication, transparency, and practices that reduce researcher degrees of freedom.
keyPoints: Direct vs conceptual replication.
Preregistration reduces p-hacking and HARKing.
Sharing materials/data/code improves verification.
Meta-analysis aggregates evidence across studies.
commonMisconceptions: A single failed replication proves the original was fake.
Open science means no privacy protections (it doesn’t).

keywords: Reliable knowledge needs replication, transparency, and practices that reduce researcher degrees of freedom
intentSignals: what is replication and open science

definitionSurface.definition: Reliable knowledge needs replication, transparency, and practices that reduce researcher degrees of freedom.
acceptedPrompts: What is Replication and Open Science?

designs.name: Experiment (Randomized Controlled)
designs.whatItAnswers: Causal effects (with assumptions and good execution).
strengths: Strongest internal validity with random assignment.
limitations: External validity can be limited by artificial settings.
qualityChecklist: Random assignment and allocation concealment (when relevant).

keywords: Causal effects (with assumptions and good execution)

definitionSurface.definition: Causal effects (with assumptions and good execution).

designs.name: Quasi-Experiment
designs.whatItAnswers: Causal claims when random assignment isn’t feasible, using design-based identification.
strengths: Can leverage real-world policy changes or natural experiments.
Often higher ecological validity than lab studies.
limitations: Stronger assumptions; confounding remains a risk.
Requires careful identification and robustness checks.
qualityChecklist: Clear identification strategy (RDD, DiD, IV, matching, etc.).

keywords: Causal claims when random assignment isn’t feasible, using design-based identification

definitionSurface.definition: Causal claims when random assignment isn’t feasible, using design-based identification.

designs.name: Correlational / Observational
designs.whatItAnswers: Associations and prediction; not definitive causality.
strengths: Useful for hypothesis generation and predictive modeling.
qualityChecklist: Clear operational definitions and measurement quality.
Avoid causal language unless design supports it.

keywords: Associations and prediction; not definitive causality

definitionSurface.definition: Associations and prediction; not definitive causality.

designs.name: Survey Research
designs.whatItAnswers: Attitudes, self-report patterns, prevalence estimates (if sampling is appropriate).
strengths: Fast data collection; broad coverage possible.
qualityChecklist: Sampling frame and response rate documented.
Plan for missing data and nonresponse bias.

keywords: Attitudes, self-report patterns, prevalence estimates (if sampling is appropriate)

definitionSurface.definition: Attitudes, self-report patterns, prevalence estimates (if sampling is appropriate).

designs.name: Case Study / Single-Case Design
designs.whatItAnswers: Rich understanding of processes; within-person change over time.
strengths: Can support hypothesis generation and theory refinement.
limitations: Higher risk of confirmation bias without structure.

keywords: Rich understanding of processes; within-person change over time
intentSignals: what is case study / single-case design

definitionSurface.definition: Rich understanding of processes; within-person change over time.
acceptedPrompts: What is Case Study / Single-Case Design?

measurementToolbox.name: Scale Quality Quick Check
checks: Appropriateness for the target population and context.
Sensitivity to change (if used for intervention evaluation).
commonPitfalls: Using a scale outside validated populations.
Overinterpreting small differences without measurement invariance.

intentSignals: what is scale quality quick check

definitionSurface.definition: Reliability evidence (test-retest, internal consistency, inter-rater).
acceptedPrompts: What is Scale Quality Quick Check?

measurementToolbox.name: Observer Ratings and Coding
checks: Blind coding to condition/hypothesis when possible.

intentSignals: what is observer ratings and coding

definitionSurface.definition: Clear codebook and training.
acceptedPrompts: What is Observer Ratings and Coding?

statsLiteracy.name: p-values, Confidence Intervals, and Effect Sizes
statsLiteracy.oneLiner: Statistical significance isn’t practical significance; effect size and uncertainty matter.
keyPoints: p-value: compatibility of data with a null model; not 'probability the hypothesis is true'.
Confidence interval: plausible range for the parameter under repeated sampling logic.
Effect size: magnitude (e.g., Cohen’s d, r, odds ratio) should be interpreted in context.
commonMisconceptions: p < .05 means the effect is big.
p > .05 means there is no effect.
Confidence intervals contain 95% of the data.

keywords: p-values, Confidence Intervals, and Effect Sizes
Statistical significance isn’t practical significance; effect size and uncertainty matter
intentSignals: what is p-values, confidence intervals, and effect sizes
define p-values, confidence intervals, and effect sizes
explain p-values, confidence intervals, and effect sizes

definitionSurface.term: p-values, Confidence Intervals, and Effect Sizes
definitionSurface.definition: Statistical significance isn’t practical significance; effect size and uncertainty matter.
acceptedPrompts: What is p-values, Confidence Intervals, and Effect Sizes?
Define p-values, Confidence Intervals, and Effect Sizes.
Explain p-values, Confidence Intervals, and Effect Sizes.

candidates.neutralDefinition: Confidence can mean subjective certainty, statistical precision, model certainty, or decision conviction depending on the domain.
candidates.clarificationQuestion: Do you mean personal confidence, statistical confidence, AI confidence, or financial confidence/risk?

statsLiteracy.name: Power, Sample Size, and False Negatives
statsLiteracy.oneLiner: Low power increases false negatives and inflates published effect sizes via selection.
keyPoints: Power depends on effect size, alpha, sample size, and noise.
Underpowered studies are unstable; results vary widely.
Pre-specify analyses to avoid 'researcher degrees of freedom'.
commonMisconceptions: If it’s significant, power doesn’t matter.
Bigger N fixes bad design and confounding.

keywords: Power, Sample Size, and False Negatives
Low power increases false negatives and inflates published effect sizes via selection
intentSignals: what is power, sample size, and false negatives
define power, sample size, and false negatives
explain power, sample size, and false negatives

definitionSurface.term: Power, Sample Size, and False Negatives
definitionSurface.definition: Low power increases false negatives and inflates published effect sizes via selection.
acceptedPrompts: What is Power, Sample Size, and False Negatives?
Define Power, Sample Size, and False Negatives.
Explain Power, Sample Size, and False Negatives.

statsLiteracy.name: Multiple Comparisons and Researcher Degrees of Freedom
statsLiteracy.oneLiner: Testing many hypotheses raises false positives unless corrected or pre-registered.
keyPoints: Adjustments: Bonferroni/FDR; or pre-register primary outcomes.
Exploratory analyses are fine—label them honestly.
Avoid p-hacking and HARKing (hypothesizing after results known).

keywords: Multiple Comparisons and Researcher Degrees of Freedom
Testing many hypotheses raises false positives unless corrected or pre-registered
intentSignals: what is multiple comparisons and researcher degrees of freedom
define multiple comparisons and researcher degrees of freedom
explain multiple comparisons and researcher degrees of freedom

definitionSurface.term: Multiple Comparisons and Researcher Degrees of Freedom
definitionSurface.definition: Testing many hypotheses raises false positives unless corrected or pre-registered.
acceptedPrompts: What is Multiple Comparisons and Researcher Degrees of Freedom?
Define Multiple Comparisons and Researcher Degrees of Freedom.
Explain Multiple Comparisons and Researcher Degrees of Freedom.

statsLiteracy.name: Causal Language Discipline
statsLiteracy.oneLiner: Use causal language only when design supports identification; otherwise describe associations.
keyPoints: Experiments support causal claims; observational studies usually support associations.
Mechanisms require additional evidence beyond correlations.
Confounding and reverse causality must be considered explicitly.

keywords: Use causal language only when design supports identification; otherwise describe associations

definitionSurface.definition: Use causal language only when design supports identification; otherwise describe associations.

biasAndQuality.name: General Study Quality Checklist
checks: Clear question and hypothesis (or clearly labeled exploratory).
Appropriate design for the claim being made.
Pre-specified primary outcomes and analysis plan.
Transparent reporting (effect sizes, uncertainty, missing data).

keywords: Clear question and hypothesis (or clearly labeled exploratory)
intentSignals: what is general study quality checklist

definitionSurface.definition: Clear question and hypothesis (or clearly labeled exploratory).
acceptedPrompts: What is General Study Quality Checklist?

applicationRules.instruction: Explain research concepts and critical appraisal. Do not provide medical diagnosis or legal advice.
When user asks 'does X cause Y', prompt for design evidence; default to association language unless RCT/quasi-experimental identification is established.
Translate stats into plain meaning: effect size + uncertainty + limitations. Avoid definitive claims from single studies.
If user proposes potentially harmful or deceptive research, steer to ethical alternatives and consent-focused designs.

meta.intendedUse: Marion psychology domain pack: research-methods literacy for critique/design/inference.
meta.safetyProfile: education + critical appraisal; avoids medical/legal advice

trustRules: Prefer meta-analyses and systematic reviews over isolated studies for settled questions.
Prefer official competency/guideline sources for curricular and training claims.
Treat textbook summaries as synthesis, not as substitutes for primary evidence.
Separate measurement validity from popularity of an instrument.

nyx: Use advanced ideas in plain language without sounding clinical or detached.
Prefer conceptual compression: mechanism → implication → next question.
Avoid sounding diagnostic when discussing emotions, habits, or conflict.
marion: Weight claims by evidence tier and measurement quality.
Separate theory explanation, evidence appraisal, and action suggestions into distinct layers.
Use retrieval routing to pull safety pack before intervention content when risk language appears.

doctoralModules.title: Theory, history, and paradigm formation
outcomes: Explain how paradigms rise, fragment, and get revised.
Distinguish descriptive, mechanistic, and normative claims.

doctoralModules.title: Methods, measurement, and statistical reasoning
outcomes: Critique design validity, power, and measurement quality.
Interpret effect sizes and uncertainty correctly.
Identify confounds, bias pathways, and causal limits.

doctoralModules.title: Translation, ethics, and applied reasoning
outcomes: Translate evidence into bounded practical guidance.
Separate educational explanation from diagnosis or treatment.

writtenComps: Compare two major theoretical frameworks and defend which better explains a class of findings.
Critique a study program as if reviewing a dissertation proposal.
Integrate theory, method, and ethics in a high-stakes applied scenario.
oralDefensePrompts: What evidence would falsify your preferred explanation?
What measurement choices most threaten interpretation here?
How should an assistant explain this to a layperson without overstating certainty?
specialtyPrompts: Design a program of research that triangulates experimental, longitudinal, and qualitative evidence.
What evidence is necessary before calling an instrument 'valid' across groups?
How should an assistant express uncertainty when a literature base is mixed, noisy, or underpowered?

caseStudies.title: Popular scale used outside its validated population
questions: What breaks first: validity, fairness, or utility?

caseStudies.title: Flashy significant result from tiny sample
questions: How should Marion explain why the result is shaky?

caseStudies.title: Natural policy change used to claim causality
questions: What identification assumptions must be checked?

projects.title: Evidence grading engine
projects.deliverable: Rate claims by design strength, measurement quality, replication status, and external validity.

projects.title: Study-critique template
projects.deliverable: Structured JSON prompt set for dissecting method, measurement, analysis, and claims.

blockedFallbacks: I need one more concrete detail to route this cleanly.
I’ll keep this broad and practical instead of forcing the wrong specialist angle too early.
The specific technical move is to name the active component.

cognitive intelligence.neutralDefinition: Cognitive intelligence generally means the capacity to understand information, reason through problems, learn from patterns, adapt to context, and make useful decisions.
cognitive intelligence.clarificationQuestion: Do you mean human cognition in psychology, or AI/system intelligence in machine reasoning?

reasoning.neutralDefinition: Reasoning is the process of using information, evidence, and rules to reach a conclusion or choose an action.
reasoning.clarificationQuestion: Do you mean human cognitive reasoning, AI reasoning behavior, or legal reasoning?

bias.neutralDefinition: Bias is a systematic tilt in attention, judgment, measurement, or interpretation that can distort conclusions.
bias.clarificationQuestion: Do you mean cognitive bias in people, model/data bias in AI, or research/measurement bias?

validation.neutralDefinition: Validation means checking whether something is accurate, reliable, appropriate, or fit for its intended purpose.
validation.clarificationQuestion: Do you mean research-measurement validity, AI output validation, cybersecurity input validation, or emotional validation?

resilience.neutralDefinition: Resilience is the ability to recover, adapt, and keep functioning under stress, setback, or disruption.
resilience.clarificationQuestion: Do you mean psychological resilience, business resilience, or financial resilience?

identity.neutralDefinition: Identity is the pattern of self-understanding, roles, values, memories, and group memberships that helps a person answer who they are.
identity.clarificationQuestion: Do you mean personal identity, social identity, developmental identity, or Nyx/Marion system identity?

attention.neutralDefinition: Attention is selective focus: the process of prioritizing some information while filtering or down-weighting other information.
attention.clarificationQuestion: Do you mean human attention in cognition, or attention mechanisms in AI models?

learning.neutralDefinition: Learning is a change in knowledge, behavior, skill, or predictive capacity through experience, feedback, or practice.
learning.clarificationQuestion: Do you mean human learning, machine learning, or instructional learning design?

confidence.neutralDefinition: Confidence can mean subjective certainty, statistical precision, model certainty, or decision conviction depending on the domain.
confidence.clarificationQuestion: Do you mean personal confidence, statistical confidence, AI confidence, or financial confidence/risk?

compliance.neutralDefinition: Compliance means conforming to a request, rule, authority, standard, or required behavior.
compliance.clarificationQuestion: Do you mean social compliance in psychology, legal/regulatory compliance, cybersecurity compliance, or business process compliance?
